{"type":"Transition","startVersion":{"querySet":11,"identity":0,"ts":"+zfo4aPPoxg="},"endVersion":{"querySet":12,"identity":0,"ts":"+zfo4aPPoxg="},"modifications":[{"type":"QueryUpdated","queryId":11,"value":[{"_creationTime":1775446223125.1816,"_id":"js79y32jk0sjm9g07cwnckh8a984a8xc","content":"Identify the claims related to artificial general intelligence that you identify from the discourse","createdAt":"2026-04-06T09:00:23.125+05:30","role":"user","threadId":"k57a8cz0tjm1n4y3vvjcrma6ns84bwjr","updatedAt":"2026-04-06T09:00:23.125+05:30"},{"_creationTime":1775446270245.792,"_id":"js7b8dz27482tybjcx7w3f189x84amf4","content":"Based on the case study’s 1,103 posts (YouTube, Twitter/X, Reddit; 2025-12-01 to 2026-02-01), I reviewed a high-engagement, AGI-focused slice of the discourse and extracted 34 discrete claims that people are making about artificial general intelligence (including timelines, capability thresholds, and institutional actions). Many high-visibility posts are promotional or opinion-only, but the claims that *do* appear cluster into a few repeating narratives:

1) Near-term AGI timeline claims (predictions)
- xAI may achieve AGI that exceeds human intelligence in 2026 (attributed to insider reporting of Elon Musk comments).
- AI could outsmart humans “this year / by 2026” (circulating as a Musk-linked warning across short-form clips and reposts).
- AGI will be achieved by 2029 (attributed to Ray Kurzweil in a video-title framing).

2) “AGI is already here” / frontier-model-is-AGI claims (classification assertions)
- Claude Opus 4.5 is AGI (asserted as a conclusion; largely treated as a debated label rather than something evidenced in-post).
- “We’ve already created AGI; it just needs RL” (appears as a quoted/relayed statement, but typically without enough sourcing in the post itself to verify who said it).

3) Large-scale transformation claims (knowledge, jobs, power)
- In 2–3 years, “90% of the world’s knowledge will be generated by AI” (attributed as a quote to NVIDIA’s CEO).
- AI will “take over language, law, and power” (framed as a warning attributed to Yuval Noah Harari, often via event/video titles).

4) Institutional/national security claims (government preparing for AGI)
- The U.S. Congress (via the fiscal 2026 NDAA) formally directed the Department of Defense to prepare for AGI, including creating an “AI Futures Steering Committee,” with specific deadlines (April 2026 formation; January 2027 report to Congress).  
  (This appears in the discourse as a “breaking” claim, but supporting sourcing is often not included in-post.)

5) Company / research lab claims about “first AGI” and robotics
- A Tokyo-based firm “Integral AI” claims it built what it calls the first AGI.
- Integral AI claims robot trials where robots learned new skills without human supervision.
- Integral AI claims its system mirrors the human neocortex (as an architecture inspiration/analogy).

6) Data + training-set scarcity claims (inputs to AGI)
- “Six frontier AI companies harvest user conversations by default” (often presented as a sweeping allegation without naming all six).
- Epoch AI projected usable human text will be exhausted by 2028.
- A Nature finding (July 2024) showed AI trained only on AI output “degenerates within five generations.”

What this suggests about the discourse
- The conversation is heavily driven by timeline forecasting and “threshold” claims (“is AGI here yet?”), more than by testable descriptions of capabilities.
- Many claims are framed as attributed quotes (“CEO said…”, “at WEF…”, “insider says…”), which makes sourcing and verification a central point of weakness in the discourse—even when the claim is influential.
- A smaller but persistent thread focuses on “data moats” and the idea that human-generated text (and user feedback) is the limiting factor for reaching AGI.

The claims viewer in the UI captures each extracted claim along with whatever corroborating or contextual sources could be found, so you can quickly see which claims are well-sourced versus mostly
